- **Metric substitution:** the headline commitment migrated from a *CV* target (operating health) to an *EPS CAGR* target (financial engineering). When a subscription business stops guiding to contract value and starts guiding to per-share earnings, the recurring engine is the problem.
- **"Adaptations / transformation"** became the dominant noun — language that signals the old playbook stopped working.
- **Free AskGartner** = revealed preference. You don't give away the AI layer for free if you're confident clients will pay more for it; you do it to *defend retention* against substitution. Management's actions contradict its "immaterial" words.
- **Levering a historically un-levered balance sheet to buy back stock** at the lows is a high-conviction bet by management that the franchise is mispriced — but it also removes the net-cash cushion if the bear case (terminal AI erosion) is right.
- The digital-markets sale ($105M) is a rounding error dressed as a "shareholder-value action" — narrative padding.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (toward financial engineering) over a DETERIORATING operating base.** Gartner is pivoting from organic-growth-led value creation to buyback-led EPS creation, financed with new debt, while the core CV engine has stalled to ~zero net growth.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** *Is the AskGartner/LLM substitution risk a manageable workflow-defense (in which case ~8.7x P/FCF on a 67% gross-margin subscription compounder is a generational fear-print) or the leading edge of structural ARPU/retention erosion (in which case a low multiple on a slowly-shrinking, debt-levered base is a value trap)?* The stable gross margin says "not yet eroding"; the free-AskGartner action and negative net new CV say "the market is right to worry." The buyback is only accretive if it's the former.

**Moat assessment.** Moat type = **intangible-brand + switching-cost** (proprietary data flywheel: 500k+ annual client conversations, multiyear no-out-clause contracts, embedded workflow in CIO/CxO decision-making). Width by the *evidence*: returns remain elevated (ROIC ~21–27%, gross margin 67.7% *stable*), which is **WIDE** on levels — but the **trend is ERODING**: CV growth 7%→1%, net new CV negative, margin reset to a lower baseline, and the company defensively giving away its AI layer. A wide-but-narrowing moat. **Secular threat = MATERIAL (not yet terminal):** generative-AI displacement of syndicated research/advisory is the live structural force — LLMs collapse the marginal cost of "good-enough" research and cap pricing power for incumbents whose moat is *workflow habit* more than hard lock-in. Gartner's hard-lock elements (proprietary primary data, executive peer network, multiyear contracts) are real and blunt the threat to "material," not "terminal" — but they do not eliminate it, and the gross margin is the tripwire to watch. The cheapness is **partly a fear-print and partly a melting-base discount**; you cannot credit it as pure mispricing.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING | MOAT: WIDE | MOAT_TREND: ERODING | SECULAR_THREAT: material

bull_thesis: Gartner (IT) is a research-subscription business where about 78% of revenue recurs (the Insights segment). After falling roughly 77% from its November 2024 all-time high of $552 to about $127, it trades at 7.8 times free cash flow (its market value divided by the cash it actually generates) and about 12-13 times trailing and forward earnings. That looks like a fear-driven price on a high-quality company: return on invested capital (the profit it earns on the money put into the business) of 21%, gross margins of 67.7%, and free cash flow that runs above net income (trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of $1.26B versus net income of about $0.8B). The bear worry is that AI chatbots will replace Gartner's research, but the live evidence points the other way. Enterprise client retention rose about 100 basis points (1 percentage point) year over year through the worst of the macro and federal-budget hit (fourth quarter of 2025); management tracks the reason for every cancellation and says churn it can tie to clients citing large language models is, in its words, essentially unmeasurable, a very small number of transactions (second and third quarters of 2025); and AskGartner, its AI assistant now rolled out to 100% of licensed users, drove substantially higher renewal rates for users than non-users (fourth quarter of 2025). So far, AI is helping retention, not leaking it. The moat rests on the brand becoming the procurement standard (the Magic Quadrant is the safe default for buying committees), on proprietary data that public-data AI cannot replicate (more than 500,000 one-on-one client conversations a year, 27,000 vendor briefings, 139,000 peer-network users), and on multiyear contracts with no early-exit clause. The slump in contract value (CV, the annualized value of subscriptions on the books) is identifiably driven by outside shocks rather than a failing product: U.S. federal and DOGE-related cuts (about 4% of contract value, roughly half retained), tariffs pushing chief financial officers to escalate buying decisions, and country-specific shocks in oil, Canada and Australia. Those headwinds are lapping: management laps the federal drag from the second quarter of 2026 and guides contract-value growth to accelerate through 2026, with adjusted earnings per share compounding more than 12% a year over three years, and latest-quarter earnings per share already up 17% year over year (the bottom may be in). Capital allocation strongly favors shareholders: $2B was repurchased in fiscal 2025 (a 6% drop in share count year over year), an investment-grade bond was issued to fund still more buyback into the trough, and the non-core Digital Markets unit was divested. At 7.8 times free cash flow, the buyback is highly accretive if earnings power simply holds. For the bull case to work, three things must hold: retention stays flat to up (so this is not the first leg of a structural leak); contract value reaccelerates as the federal drag laps in 2026; and the 23.5% margin baseline holds. If they do, fair value sits well north of $180 on a normalized 15-18 times earnings or 12 times free cash flow, with the stock re-rating as growth visibly turns up.
bear_thesis: The bear case is that Gartner is a structurally impaired franchise whose moat is workflow habit and brand-as-reference, exactly the layer generative AI erodes, and that the chart already reflects it. A 77% fall from the all-time high is not a temporary air-pocket; it is the early stage of a permanent re-rating of syndicated research toward a lower valuation. The warning signs come from management itself. Contract value (CV, the annualized value of subscriptions on the books) growth has decelerated from 7% (first quarter of 2025) to 3% (third quarter of 2025) to 1% on a currency-neutral basis (first quarter of 2026 actual), despite a year of adaptations supposedly yielding results, so the promised inflection keeps not arriving. The margin baseline was cut to 23.5%, which the chief financial officer explicitly called the new baseline (fourth quarter of 2025), conceding that the cost of competing (more analysts, more content, AI tooling) has structurally risen. AskGartner, the AI assistant, is bundled at no extra charge, so it is pure cost with no monetization and caps revenue per account just as the 3-4% price increases are already the ceiling. The answer that AI-driven churn is unmeasurable is a non-falsifiable deflection of the exact risk the whole thesis turns on; when management will not quantify it, assume it is being managed, not measured. Fiscal-2025 earnings per share fell 39.7% year over year, and the seven-of-seven streak of earnings beats is beating a lowered bar (they beat a base that had halved). Enterprise client count is declining, waved off as small-tech-vendor churn that conveniently spares the core. Most dangerous, they are levering up (the investment-grade bond) to buy back stock into a falling knife; if the base is melting, that is financial engineering into a value trap that removes the balance-sheet cushion (net debt is now $1.59B). Return on equity of 203.7% is a treasury-stock artifact, not real returns. What confirms the bear: any quarter where retention rolls over, contract value stays below 3%, or pricing concedes, at which point a cheap 7.8 times free cash flow re-rates to 5-6 times on a shrinking base and the buyback compounds the loss per share.
consensus_delta: For a deep-value name, the question is whether the market is pricing in terminal decline at a franchise that still has stable or growing earnings power. Here the market is looking the wrong way in both directions, which partly offsets. The sell-side price target ($176, about 38% above the price) and consensus forward earnings-per-share growth of 12.2% still model a clean cyclical reacceleration, the old assumption that Gartner always compounds back to 12-16% contract-value growth, which the live data does not yet support: contract value (the annualized value of subscriptions on the books) grew just 1% on a currency-neutral basis in the first quarter of 2026, the pace of analyst target cuts is running at 24%, and the margin baseline was cut to 23.5%. That consensus is too optimistic on the path. At the same time the price (7.8 times free cash flow, about 12 times earnings, 77% off the all-time high) is pricing in structural AI-displacement decline that the evidence does not corroborate: retention up 100 basis points (1 percentage point), free cash flow above net income, AskGartner raising renewal rates, and AI-citing churn described as unmeasurable. That is too pessimistic on the terminal outcome. So where we disagree with the market is real but two-sided and self-cancelling: you are long a mispriced long-term valuation but short a too-rosy near-term growth path. The exact wrong assumption to fade is that contract value snaps back to double digits in 2026 (it will not; management itself moved the 12-16% goal to 2027-2028 and beyond), and the exact wrong price assumption is that this is the next linear-media or Adobe-class collapse (the data says the damage is material, not terminal). Net: cheap, but not a slam-dunk mispricing, a quality franchise marked down for a real-but-unconfirmed structural threat, with a too-optimistic growth consensus capping the near-term recovery.
forcing_function: The main announced catalyst, the Digital Markets divestiture (Capterra, Software Advice and GetApp sold to G2), has already happened and is immaterial: it closed February 5, 2026 for about $104.8M net (a roughly $6.1M pre-tax gain), about 1% of the roughly $9.7B market value, so there is no re-rating left in it. That leaves no discrete, hard-dated catalyst that forces the re-rating; for this deep-value name, what drives the re-rating is operational, not an event. The events that would push the market to re-rate are the quarterly contract-value and retention reports. (Contract value, or CV, is the annualized value of subscriptions on the books.) The second-quarter 2026 call (around August 2026) is the first quarter that laps the U.S. federal and DOGE headwind, making it the cleanest read on whether contract value excluding the federal piece reaccelerates as guided: a clean acceleration there is the catalyst the bull is waiting for, and a fourth straight quarter of sub-3% contract value is the bear's. Secondary to that, the continued $2B-a-year buyback steadily shrinks the share count, a slow-burn per-share tailwind rather than a dated event. There is no proxy fight, activist, or asset-sale catalyst pending. Because the one dated catalyst has already fired and the re-rating now hinges on a single shared question (does AI impair the base), this is a grind on fundamentals, not a special situation: it lacks both an independent driver to resolve it and a hard-dated catalyst inside the window we care about.
valley_of_death: The risks over the next three to nine months are real but not balance-sheet-threatening. First, contract-value growth (the annualized value of subscriptions on the books) may stay stuck below 3% through the first half of 2026: management laps the federal headwind only from the second quarter of 2026 and guides acceleration throughout 2026, so if the first one or two reports do not show the turn, the stock has no near-term catalyst and the turnaround-is-working story breaks. Second, the margin step-down to a 23.5% baseline is now locked in, so earnings growth depends purely on revenue reaccelerating with no help from margins; any revenue disappointment flows straight through. Third, the buyback funded by the investment-grade bond adds leverage (net debt of $1.59B) into a falling stock; this is not a debt-maturity crisis (the Altman Z-score, a bankruptcy-risk gauge, is 3.2, comfortably solvent), but it converts a former net-cash cushion into modest leverage just as the operating outlook is uncertain. Fourth, Consulting (project-based work, down 13% in the fourth quarter of 2025) and small-tech-vendor churn keep enterprise client count declining, a recurring negative headline each quarter. There is no cash-burn hump or refinancing wall (free cash flow is $1.26B and solidly positive), so the risky window ahead is about the valuation and the narrative (no catalyst plus decelerating contract value) rather than solvency. The real tail risk is a single bad retention report that turns the AI fear from unmeasurable into measured, which would mark down the long-term valuation hard.
positioning_washout: There is limited forced-selling pressure here. This is not a yield or dividend name (Gartner pays no dividend and returns all capital through buybacks), so there is no income-fund base that a dividend cut could push out. The selling that has already happened is the unwinding of its growth, momentum and quality-factor following: a former premium-multiple growth-at-a-reasonable-price compounder (over 30 times earnings at the all-time high) has been ejected from growth mandates as contract value (the annualized value of subscriptions on the books) decelerated and the multiple compressed to about 12 times earnings. That forced selling is largely behind the stock, which sits 77% off its high, at 0% of its 52-week range, and 36% below its 200-day moving average, technically about as washed out as it gets. The remaining mechanical risk is index or quality-factor rebalancing if margins and return on invested capital keep sliding (the cut to a 23.5% margin baseline could trim it from quality screens). But with no yield base and an already-extreme washout, there is little incremental forced supply left to wait for, the capitulation is mostly spent, which argues for entering rather than waiting. And the company itself is the marginal buyer (the $2B-a-year buyback), absorbing shares.
moderator_conclusion: Gartner is a genuinely cheap, genuinely high-quality recurring-subscription franchise (7.8 times free cash flow, 21% return on invested capital, free cash flow above net income, retention up 100 basis points) that has been cut 77% from its all-time high on a real-but-unconfirmed secular threat (AI displacing syndicated research) layered on top of an identifiably outside-driven slump in contract value (the annualized value of subscriptions on the books) from the federal and DOGE cuts and tariffs. The forensic record is solid (we rate management's credibility 4 out of 5, with seven of seven earnings beats, no insider selling, and genuine free-cash-flow quality), but the business is turning: a former double-digit compounder is now defending its base and returning all its cash through leverage-funded buybacks, with contract value decelerated to about 1% and the margin baseline cut to a permanent 23.5%. Against the current market environment this name scores poorly on the things that environment rewards: its one dated catalyst (the Digital Markets sale) has fired and was immaterial; there is no hard-dated, in-window special situation; and the whole thesis hinges on one shared question (does AI impair syndicated research), exactly the concentration of resolution drivers to be wary of (the book is already flagged for AI-displacement concentration, with Gartner a named anchor alongside Adobe and Globant). The environment favors underfollowed, structurally special situations (distressed names first, then spin-offs, then forced-sellers) and deprioritizes single-question theses, which actively demotes this: it is a well-covered large-cap value grind, not an underfollowed special situation. The recommendation is to wait for the wash-out: the value is real and the selling is largely spent, but with no catalyst and a too-optimistic growth consensus still to disappoint, the right move is to wait for the second-quarter 2026 report (around August 2026, the first quarter to lap the federal drag) to confirm contract value is inflecting before sizing a position. Entering now means buying a fundamentals grind with the AI tail unhedged and no catalyst to pull the re-rating forward. The specific signal to wait for: contract value excluding the federal piece reaccelerating above roughly 5-6% (the bull trigger) or retention rolling over (the bear confirmation), whichever prints first. Our conviction is a modest 3 out of 5.
sop_breakdown: We anchor the reconciled base case on earnings and free cash flow (which binds more tightly than the more optimistic sales-multiple, business-line-by-business-line valuation for a name facing an eroding moat and AI displacement). Sum-of-the-parts means valuing each business line separately. Normalized trailing-twelve-month earnings per share of about $10.1 at about 17 times (a recurring-but-decelerating subscription franchise, below structural peers MSCI and FactSet at roughly 25-30 times but well above a terminal-decline 12 times) gives about $172. Cross-checked against 12 times free cash flow on trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of $1.26B less net debt of $1.59B, that is about $177 a share. A more conservative line-by-line sum-of-the-parts (Insights $5.1B at 4.0 times, Conferences $0.6B at 2.5 times, Consulting $0.55B at 1.2 times = enterprise value $22.6B minus $1.59B = $275 a share on sales) is deliberately not used as the anchor, because the eroding moat trend and capped revenue per account make a full recurring-research sales multiple unsupportable. Base sum-of-the-parts fair value is about $175 a share. Note: the third-party segment data was a defective stub that omitted the core Insights line, so the sum-of-the-parts was rebuilt on segments verified from the 10-K filing.
risk_reward: The risk and reward is roughly symmetric to favorable. Upside to fair value of about $175 from about $127 is about 38% (matching the 38% sell-side target of $176.10). Downside to the break point of about $120-130 (the bear's earnings and free-cash-flow anchor, near the current price) is about 2-6%. So that is about 38% up versus about 5% down to the bear floor it already sits near, roughly a 6-to-1 ratio at this price. But the bear's true long-term tail (a confirmed AI-displacement re-rating to about $90-100 on 10-11 times a shrinking base) is the real downside and is not trivial. Stated honestly: about 38% up to fair value versus about 20-25% down to the structural-decline tail, roughly 1.5-to-2-to-1 once that long-term tail is respected.
peer_comps_note: The right comparison set is subscription research and data platforms, MSCI at about 30 times free cash flow, FactSet at about 25 times, Verisk at about 25 times, and S&P Global at about 22 times, against which Gartner at 7.8 times free cash flow looks deeply cheap. I credit this only partly, not fully. Those peers carry rising or stable revenue per account and no live AI-substitution signal, whereas Gartner's contract value (the annualized value of subscriptions on the books) is decelerating to about 1% and its margin baseline was cut, so Gartner deserves a discount to the 22-30 times group; but a 7.8 times free cash flow, roughly 12 times earnings multiple is below even a marked-down research level and prices in terminal decline that retention (up 100 basis points) and free cash flow above net income do not yet corroborate. The discount is specific to Gartner (decelerating contract value plus AI fear), not a shared sector or regulatory factor, so part of it is a genuine single-name mispricing, but the eroding moat trend caps how much of the gap counts as real opportunity rather than a justified quality discount. The anchor multiple used is about 17 times earnings or 12 times free cash flow (a marked-down research level, not the stale 22-30 times peer level).
interrogator_dossier:
A deep dive on management and the setup, Gartner, Inc. (IT)

Signal type: deep value. Sector (as the screener tags it): Industrials (this is a misclassification, Gartner is a subscription research and advisory franchise, not an industrial company). Transcripts reviewed: first quarter of 2025 through fourth quarter of 2025 (the fiscal-2025 cycle).

1. The story over the year

Across the four fiscal-2025 calls (May, August and November 2025, and February 2026), Gartner's story moved clearly and consistently from a resilient compounder pausing to a business structurally transforming under pressure.

First quarter of 2025 (May): the frame was resilience plus temporary macro. Contract value (CV, the annualized value of subscriptions on the books) grew 7%; management leaned on a list of past shocks survived (COVID, the tech bubble, 40-year-high inflation, a ground war in Europe) and positioned the U.S. federal and DOGE hit as a contained problem worth about 4% of contract value. The promise: reaccelerate contract-value growth to the 12-16% target when the macro environment returns to normal. The thesis was wait for normalization.

Second quarter of 2025 (August): the pivot to AI as the dominant lens begins. Hall opens with two things: AI is an important opportunity for Gartner, and we are making adaptations that give us a clear path back to double-digit growth. This is the first call where management feels compelled to get ahead of the AI-substitution worry, rolling out AskGartner (its AI assistant), citing CEO confidence at recessionary levels, and renaming the Research segment as Business and Technology Insights (Insights). Renaming a flagship segment mid-crisis is itself a signal.

Third quarter of 2025 (November): contract-value growth decelerates to 3% (6% excluding the federal piece). The story is now that operational adaptations are starting to yield results, engagement up, retention up about 100 basis points (1 percentage point), more than $1B in buyback. The AI defense hardens: 6,000 AI documents, 1,000 use cases, AskGartner rolled out to 100% of users. Notably the offensive 12-16% target is now softened to double-digit growth over the medium term.

Fourth quarter of 2025 (February 2026): full transformation framing, the world is changing more than ever, this is a huge opportunity for us. Margin guidance steps down to a new 23.5% baseline (from about 25%), $2B was repurchased in fiscal 2025, an investment-grade bond was issued to lever up for more buyback, and the Digital Markets divestiture was announced. The story has quietly shifted from a growth franchise to a cash-return franchise buying time for an operational turnaround.

Quietly dropped: the crisp 12-16% contract value when macro normalizes. Newly introduced: AskGartner, the Insights rebrand, the four-dimension transformation (impact, volume, timeliness, user experience), and leverage-funded buyback.

2. Management claims versus the financials

Clear path back to double-digit growth (claimed second through fourth quarter of 2025): the numbers show contract-value growth of 7%, then 3% in the third quarter, then 1% on a currency-neutral basis for total contract value (first-quarter-2026 actual, $5.3B), with revenue up 3.7% year over year and decelerating. Verdict: poorer than narrated. Contract value is decelerating, not inflecting; double digits is a hope, not a trend.

Resilient, results ahead of expectations, a beat every quarter (claimed across all calls): earnings per share beat in seven of seven quarters (100%), but fiscal-2025 earnings per share was 9.65, down 39.7% year over year, and net income went from $1.25B (fiscal 2024) to $729M (fiscal 2025). Verdict: mixed. The beats are real but against a collapsing base; the 40% earnings drop swamps them, they beat a lowered bar. (Note: the fiscal-2024 earnings per share of 16.0 and net income of $1.25B were inflated by one-off items, so normalized trailing-twelve-month earnings per share is about 10.1.)

We generate free cash flow well in excess of net income (claimed across all calls): trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of $1.26B versus trailing-twelve-month net income of about $0.8B, a free-cash-flow margin of 18.1%, and 7.8 times free cash flow. Verdict: true, and the load-bearing fact. Free-cash-flow conversion above net income is the genuine quality marker and the real value anchor, not reported earnings per share.

Repurchased $2B of stock in 2025, eager to repurchase aggressively, price-sensitive (claimed third and fourth quarter of 2025): more than $1B in the third quarter alone, $2B for the full year, share count down 6% year over year, and they issued investment-grade debt to fund it. Net debt is $1.59B (recomputed). Verdict: true but financed. They are levering to shrink the share count into a falling stock, accretive if earnings power is stable, value-destructive if it is structurally eroding.

Margins expand through natural operating leverage (claimed first quarter of 2025): margin guidance was instead stepped down to a 23.5% new baseline by the fourth quarter of 2025. Verdict: contradicted within the year; the first-quarter operating-leverage promise was reversed to a lower baseline by the fourth quarter.

Return on equity 203.7%, return on invested capital 21.0% (from the financials): return on equity is distorted by a buyback-shrunk equity base (near-zero or negative book value); return on invested capital of 21% is real. Verdict: return on equity is a financial-engineering artifact (treasury stock), ignore it; return on invested capital of 21% is the honest, still-premium return, but it is falling from prior-year levels as earnings per share halved.

Net: the numbers support a high-free-cash-flow, high-return-on-invested-capital subscription franchise in a mid-cycle air-pocket, not a broken business, but they do not support the clear path back to double-digit growth claim, which is unproven and decelerating in the live data.

3. Tone and confidence over time, declining (offensive to defensive)

First quarter of 2025 (confident and offensive): we will continue to be agile, invest to support long-term sustained double-digit growth; past-shocks-survived bravado.

Second quarter of 2025 (defensive pivot): measures of CEO confidence fell to recessionary levels, among the fastest drops ever recorded; 78% of CEOs implementing cost-cutting. The tone shifts to explaining headwinds and getting ahead of the AI-substitution worry.

Third quarter of 2025 (cautiously reassuring): adaptations are starting to yield results, but the proof points are leading indicators (engagement, pipeline up double digits), not booked contract value, which fell to 3%.

Fourth quarter of 2025 (resigned but reframing): executives have responded by slowing and deferring everything possible, this makes for a much tougher selling environment; some say the rate of change will never be this slow again; the margin baseline was lowered. The language is now about surviving a structurally harder sell and returning cash, a defensive posture dressed as opportunity.

Guidance got vaguer on growth (the 12-16% target was loosened to medium term) while getting more precise on capital return (exact buyback dollars). That asymmetry, vague on the top line and precise on financial engineering, is a classic late-cycle defensive tell.

4. How much to trust the guidance, medium

The seven-of-seven (100%) earnings-beat rate is genuine and supports near-term sandbagging discipline (Safian repeatedly frames operating-expense guidance as our normal prudent approach, and results consistently came in ahead of expectations). On near-term numbers, credibility is high.

But the strategic guidance has a credibility gap: the first-quarter-2025 operating-leverage-to-margin-expansion promise was reversed to a lower 23.5% baseline by the fourth quarter of 2025, and the 12-16% contract-value target was pushed out every call (2027, 2028 and beyond). Forward-looking strategic promises have not been delivered on the implied timeline.

Net reliability: medium, trust them on the quarter, discount them on the multi-year reacceleration.

5. Where analysts pressed

The single recurring pressure point, asked four different ways across the year, is AI substitution risk, and management's answers are the most evasive part of the transcripts:

Second quarter of 2025 (Haas): what percentage of folks are citing usage of a publicly available large language model and not consuming the Gartner subscription? Hall: it is not material, it is essentially unmeasurable.

Third quarter of 2025 (Sabadra): how often does a large language model come up as an alternative to Gartner? Hall: extremely small, a very small number of transactions.

Third quarter of 2025 (Bibb): pressing whether AskGartner is a separate charge or engagement, confirmed bundled into the base package (it generates no incremental revenue, a defensive retention tool, a cost, not a growth lever).

The essentially unmeasurable and very small answers are non-falsifiable deflections. When management cannot or will not quantify the precise risk the entire bear thesis rests on, that is itself a yellow flag. The second pressure point, declining enterprise client count (first the technology-leaders group, then the business-functions group), is attributed entirely to small-tech-vendor churn, which is plausible but conveniently spares the core franchise from blame.

6. Warning signs and reassuring signs

Warning signs: contract-value growth decelerated 7% to 3% to 1% currency-neutral despite adaptations yielding results; the margin baseline was cut from about 25% to 23.5%, admitted by Safian in the fourth quarter of 2025 as structural, not cyclical; the segment was rebranded mid-crisis (Research to Insights), a cosmetic move under pressure; they are levering up (the investment-grade bond) to buy back stock into a 77% drawdown, a high-conviction capital return but one that removes the balance-sheet cushion if the air-pocket is structural; the AI-substitution question is met with unmeasurable or very small, an unquantified deflection of the core risk; and AskGartner is bundled with no incremental revenue, an AI defense that is pure cost.

Reassuring signs: free-cash-flow conversion above net income consistently, with trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of $1.26B at 7.8 times free cash flow, genuinely cheap; return on invested capital of 21%, gross margin of 67.7% (stable), and Insights about 78% recurring subscription with multiyear, no-early-exit contracts (Safian, first quarter of 2025: true multiyear contracts, no out clauses); retention up about 100 basis points year over year (fourth quarter of 2025) even through the worst of the macro and federal hit; seven of seven quarters beaten, with latest-quarter earnings per share up 17% year over year (trailing-twelve-month diluted earnings per share of 10.12), so the bottom may be in; and a 6% year-over-year share-count reduction, which makes the per-share math powerful if earnings power holds.

7. Hidden signals

The Insights rebrand is not purely cosmetic: by the third quarter of 2025 they also moved most Insights non-subscription revenue to other revenue, purifying the headline Insights segment to almost 100% recurring. This is management curating the metric investors watch to emphasize recurring quality precisely as growth stalls, defensible, but a signaling choice.

Capital-allocation tone shift: from price-sensitive, opportunistic, disciplined buyback (first quarter) to issuing debt to fund $2B (fourth quarter). They are telling you they think the stock is cheap with conviction, but also that organic growth is not absorbing the cash.

The new baseline is 23.5% (Safian, fourth quarter of 2025), management itself reclassifying the margin step-down as permanent, not a dip to be recovered, a subtle but important admission that the cost of competing (more analysts, more content, AI tooling) has structurally risen.

AI framed offensively but deployed defensively: every AI mention is opportunity, but every concrete AI product (AskGartner) is bundled and aimed at retention, not new pricing or revenue per account. The gap between the offensive language and the defensive deployment is the tell.

8. Capital-allocation verdict, turning (compounder to cash-return and defend-the-base)

The trajectory is turning: a former double-digit growth compounder is mid-transition to a defend-the-recurring-base, return-all-cash franchise, funding aggressive buyback with new leverage while it waits for an operational turnaround that has so far shown up only in leading indicators, not booked contract value.

The single most important thing to weigh before sizing: is the 77% drawdown a fear-print on a temporarily air-pocketed but durable subscription franchise (7.8 times free cash flow of a 21%-return-on-invested-capital, 78%-recurring compounder), or the early stage of an AI-displacement re-rating of syndicated research toward a structurally lower terminal valuation? The buyback-funded leverage means the downside is not symmetric, if the base is melting, they are levering into a value trap.

Economic moat: type, intangible brand plus switching cost (the Gartner brand as the safe standard-of-reference in enterprise IT procurement, the Magic Quadrant as the buying-committee default, plus multiyear no-early-exit subscriptions and embedded peer-network and analyst-access habit). Width: the level is wide (67.7% gross margin, 21% return on invested capital, retention up 100 basis points), but the evidence shows it is eroding at the margin, contract-value growth collapsing to about 1%, the margin baseline cut to 23.5%, and pricing power capped (3-4% price increases are the ceiling, AskGartner bundled with no incremental monetization). A high-but-no-longer-rising return profile with a lowered margin baseline is a moat that is narrowing, not widening.

Secular threat (the test of long-term value): the structural force is AI displacement of syndicated research and advisory, large language models collapse the marginal cost of good-enough research output, capping the pricing power of a franchise whose moat is workflow habit and brand-as-reference rather than hard data lock-in. This is the textbook AI-displacement victim layer (syndicated research and advisory). The threat is real and material, but not yet demonstrably terminal: the moat is brand, trust and independence (the Magic Quadrant as a procurement-defensibility artifact, proprietary 500,000-plus annual client conversations, the conference network) that large language models trained on public data cannot trivially replicate, and retention is rising, not falling. The cheapness is therefore a partial fear-print over a genuinely threatened base, neither a clean mispricing nor a confirmed value trap. The evidence (rising retention, free cash flow above net income, no measurable AI-driven churn per management) tilts it toward material-not-terminal, but the eroding moat trend and lowered margin baseline mean it cannot be underwritten as wide.

Summary read: management credibility 4 out of 5; trajectory turning; moat narrow but real and eroding; secular threat material.
